party_alliance:
You aligned most with the Alliance Party. Based on your answers, your views on equality, climate, and integrated education are closest to Alliance's actual voting record over the past four years.

Their record on equality matches yours most closely. In 2022, Alliance secured the passage of the Integrated Education Act, a concrete legislative win for non-denominational schooling. Justice Minister Naomi Long has spent this term advancing hate crime legislation and pushing for a conversion therapy ban, both of which remain in progress but show a consistent direction.

But here is where they may diverge from you. Alliance has not pursued rent control measures, taking a cautious approach to housing intervention that prioritises supply over price regulation. If housing affordability is important to you, that gap is worth weighing.

The Alliance Party holds 17 seats at Stormont and is led by Naomi Long, who has served as Justice Minister throughout this term. As a cross-community, non-sectarian party, Alliance played a central stabilising role in restoring the Executive after the 2022 to 2024 collapse.

In the next term, watch for Alliance to push conversion therapy legislation, expand integrated education, and press for stronger climate targets under the 2022 Climate Change Act framework. They are likely to remain a kingmaker in any future coalition.

party_dup:
You aligned most with the Democratic Unionist Party. Based on your answers, your views on economic policy and maintaining Northern Ireland's constitutional position within the United Kingdom are closest to the DUP's actual record.

Their record on economic competitiveness matches yours most closely. The DUP has consistently advocated for lower corporation tax, pro-business policies, and protecting Northern Ireland's place in the UK internal market. Following the Windsor Framework negotiations, the party secured mitigations to post-Brexit trading arrangements that had divided the unionist community.

But here is where they may diverge from you. The DUP's 21-month boycott of Stormont from 2022 to 2024 left the Assembly suspended, during which time no legislation could pass and public services went unfunded. If functional devolved government matters to you, that record is worth weighing.

The Democratic Unionist Party holds 25 seats at Stormont and is led by Gavin Robinson. After returning to the Executive in February 2024, the DUP holds ministerial positions and resumed its role as the largest unionist party in government.

In the next term, watch for the DUP to continue testing the Windsor Framework arrangements, press for greater Westminster control over Northern Ireland's finances, and resist any deepening of cross-border economic ties.

party_sinn_fein:
You aligned most with Sinn Féin. Based on your answers, your views on Irish language rights, equality, welfare protection, and cross-border cooperation are closest to Sinn Féin's actual voting record.

Their record on equality and language rights matches yours most closely. The Identity and Language Act 2022 delivered statutory recognition for the Irish language after decades of campaigning. Sinn Féin also oversaw the commissioning of abortion services across all Health and Social Care Trusts, and brokered cost-of-living support packages during the energy price crisis.

But here is where they may diverge from you. Academic selection at 11 remains in place. Despite this being a long-standing Sinn Féin commitment, no legislative change has been made to the grammar school system during this term. Rent controls were advocated but not delivered.

Sinn Féin holds 27 seats at Stormont, making it the largest party, and is led by Michelle O'Neill, who became First Minister in February 2024, the first republican to hold that office in Northern Ireland's history.

In the next term, watch for Sinn Féin to advance a border poll discussion, pursue further housing reform, and push for deeper all-island economic integration in energy and infrastructure.

party_uup:
You aligned most with the Ulster Unionist Party. Based on your answers, your views on healthcare, pragmatic unionism, and economic policy within the United Kingdom are closest to the UUP's actual record.

Their record on healthcare matches yours most closely. Robin Swann served as Health Minister throughout the restored Executive, overseeing additional funding of around 135 million pounds per year to tackle waiting lists, and brokering pay deals that restored parity with England after lengthy industrial action. It was practical, crisis-focused governance.

But here is where they may diverge from you. The UUP has not pursued major structural reforms. Academic selection remains defended, corporation tax devolution has made no progress, and the party has taken a generally conservative position on equality and identity legislation.

The Ulster Unionist Party holds 9 seats at Stormont and is led by Doug Beattie, who has taken a more moderate and pragmatic approach to unionism compared to the DUP and TUV. The UUP returned to the Executive in 2024 without the prolonged boycott that defined the DUP's strategy.

In the next term, watch for the UUP to focus on health system reform, rural economic policy, and maintaining a constructive but distinct unionist voice in the Assembly.

party_sdlp:
You aligned most with the SDLP. Based on your answers, your views on legacy justice, equality, cross-border cooperation, and social democratic values are closest to the SDLP's record as Official Opposition.

Their record on equality and environmental advocacy matches yours most closely. The SDLP successfully moved an Assembly motion calling for a conversion therapy ban in 2024, has been a consistent voice on Lough Neagh water quality and environmental protection, and has pushed for stronger hate crime legislation throughout this term.

But here is where they may diverge from you. The SDLP chose not to enter the Executive in 2022, limiting their direct policy delivery. Most of their flagship commitments, including the Magee University expansion and a 13,000-home social housing plan, have either partially progressed or remain undelivered. If you want direct power over outcomes, their opposition role limits that.

The SDLP holds 8 seats at Stormont and is led by Colum Eastwood, who also serves as an MP at Westminster. The party operates as both an Assembly opposition and a Westminster parliamentary force.

In the next term, watch for the SDLP to press for legacy justice reform, continue campaigning on housing and regional development, and deepen cooperation with Fianna Fáil in the south.

party_pbp:
You aligned most with People Before Profit. Based on your answers, your views on housing intervention, workers' rights, healthcare reform, and climate policy are closest to People Before Profit's record as a single-MLA opposition.

Their record on housing matches yours most closely. Gerry Carroll introduced the People's Housing Bill in 2024 and 2025, the most ambitious rent control and no-fault eviction ban legislation proposed in this Assembly term. While it was not passed, it set the terms of the housing debate and forced the issue onto the Executive agenda.

But here is where they may diverge from you. With only one Assembly seat, People Before Profit has no ministerial power and limited ability to deliver legislation directly. If executive authority matters to you, their structural constraints are significant. Most of their commitments, including energy nationalisation and free public transport, remain undelivered at Stormont level.

People Before Profit holds 1 seat at Stormont, held by Gerry Carroll in West Belfast. The party takes a socialist, anti-sectarian position and does not engage in the traditional nationalist or unionist community politics that dominate the Assembly.

In the next term, watch for People Before Profit to continue leading on housing reform and workers' rights, particularly if their seat count increases after the next election.

party_tuv:
You aligned most with the Traditional Unionist Voice. Based on your answers, your views on the constitutional position of Northern Ireland, economic policy, and opposition to cross-border political structures are closest to TUV's record.

Their record on constitutional opposition matches yours most closely. Jim Allister has been the most consistent and legally informed critic of the Windsor Framework in the Assembly, arguing that the arrangement places Northern Ireland under EU single market rules without democratic consent. His scrutiny contributed to the pressure that influenced the 2023 renegotiation.

But here is where they may diverge from you. TUV has never held a ministerial position and operates in permanent opposition to the power-sharing structures that make devolved government function in Northern Ireland. With one MLA, the party's ability to deliver policy outcomes is structurally nil. If you want government to work, the TUV model requires a fundamental restructuring of how Northern Ireland is governed.

The Traditional Unionist Voice holds 1 seat at Stormont, held by Jim Allister, a King's Counsel and the party's founder and leader. Allister is widely regarded as the most forensic debater in the Assembly.

In the next term, watch for TUV to continue challenging the Windsor Framework legally and politically, and to press for stronger unionist coordination ahead of the next Assembly election.
